I'm not per se against nuclear - as a last resort - but I have the impression that there is more potential in renewables than many think. You need to be smart about it, cooperate and implement the *combination* of different renewable energy sources, energy storage and flexible power grids. Once this is in place, a steady 100% renewable power supply is technically feasible and even economically atractive. Then you can cleanly electrify mobility, heating, cooling. Technologically we are not that far away. Perhaps keep existing nuclear for some time as a safety buffer...

More than 100% of the *observed global warming* is due to human activities. Without human activities it would more likely than not have cooled. Somewhat oversimplified: You first need to compensate for that cooling and then get to the part that produces observed warming. The two main components of human activities are emitting greenhouse gases and producing tiny airborne particles (aerosols). The former warm, that latter cool. So greenhouse gases are responsible for a lot more than 100% of the observed warming. Without the aerosol cooling the observed warming would be a few tenth of a degree Celsius higher. That is actually the part that produces most of the uncertainties, not the greenhouse gases. It is hard to determine how much aerosols themselves cool and how they interact with clouds. Furthermore, we do not accurately know the aerosol mix and concentration before the industrial revolution.

Ok, I could perhaps have been more explicit about their "RES-I". They say: >RES, namely wind power and solar PV, are characterised by intermittent electricity generation. and >The increase in the installed capacities of intermittent renewable energy sources (RES-I) So the RES-I category in the quote most definitely includes wind power, not just bioenergy and geothermal. They reiterate this in the conclusion. So: 1. the paper clearly states renewables including wind replaces electricity production from coal 2. the paper also clearly states that more natural gas plant CAPACITY is required, but I don't see it anywhere stating fossil fuel CONSUMPTION increases. Having low-cost wind replace coal, backed up by a peaking gas plant seems like a pretty common sense solution to me.

That's a reasonable explanation. So let's explore something for a moment. Your initial reply to me implied (though I might be reading too much into this) that you oppose further tests. One of the things I've always found remarkable about this approach to mitigation is the vehement opposition to further testing from certain environmental NGOs (ENGOs). (These are generally the same groups that oppose nuclear power.) How much of your opposition (and I'll expand "your" to include your peers) driven by pressure from these groups and how much is driven by rational scientific concern? I ask because, so far, none of the experiments seem to have had the sorts of negative effects you describe.

Ah. Very fair. I would agree with that. Most Texas dems are not pro gun but, I think there are enough of us that are moderate and running for office that we "might" be ok. Texas is so large with so much rural area, implementing strict gun regulation would be a disservice everywhere in the state but the urban cores. I'm running for office myself and while I personally don't think stricter background checks will do anything to keep illegal guns off the street, I honestly don't have a problem with it. I have to get a background check already anyways. Might as well make the change there and not to something that would actually infringe 2A rights.

It’s about more than the fact that none are infallible. What it really comes down to is that they flat-out cannot achieve their stated goals, even partly. There are such huge holes in these offerings. What murder prevention is there in creating a trail by which you can trace a gun to its last known legal owner, for example? As I’ve said, the majority of people who use guns for murder don’t obtain the guns legally. It would do no good to trace their a gun—assuming the gun is even recovered, and they aren’t always—to the last owner of record. He wouldn’t be the one who used it in the murder. Maybe it was stolen from him in an unsolved burglary. And even if most law-abiding gun owners wouldn’t be “losing” their guns, anyone who was inclined to criminality (and even those who aren’t) could escape having the government have a list of his guns by declaring them lost or stolen. From there, he can sell them on the black market, or secretly keep them after a new law bans possession of them.

Author also is all’s about how allowing abortion would allow infanticide (which isn’t the case) which in no way proves people are okay with it. It’s like pro-lifers trying to prove life starts at conception but then realising that if that were the case you can’t jail pregnant women anymore, etc, and then is coming to the conclusion that pro-lifers want to prevent pregnant women from going to jail even if they did a crime. I believe you should be able to abort at any point. Bodily autonomy is a human right that we all have. We can not be forced to or forced not to use our body as we choose. But i am also against the death of fetuses. Anytime you take the life of a human you should face consequences. Because of that, while i support abortion at any point, i much much prefer them to be performed after the fetus has reached validity, so it can live through the abortion. So, i dont want to draw ANY line, but if i had to, it would be before validity
